summary: Joel Spolsky announces a call for nominations for 'The Best Software Writing, Volume II,' a follow-up to his successful first anthology of great software development writing from around the web. He recaps his motivation: the software world is drowning in poorly written technical books and low-quality online commentary. The first volume was a hit, validating his belief that the community needs and wants better writing about software. He invites readers to nominate their favorite articles via a discussion group before the April 15th deadline.
key_points: The first volume of 'The Best Software Writing' was successful enough to warrant a sequel
Spolsky identifies three categories of bad software writing: bloated multi-author technical books, pretentious academic-style OOP books, and low-quality online rants
The anthology model curates the best writing from across the web rather than commissioning new content
Community participation drives the selection process through open nominations
money_quotes: The software development world desperately needs better writing.
If I have to read another 2000 page book about some class library written by 16 separate people in broken ESL, I'm going to flip out.

If I see another hardback book about object oriented models written with dense faux-academic pretentiousness, I'm not going to shelve it any more in the Fog Creek library: it's going right in the recycle bin.
If I have to read another spirited attack on Microsoft's buggy code by an enthusiastic nine year old Trekkie on Slashdot, I might just poke my eyes out with a sharpened pencil.
Stop it, stop it, stop it!
key_insight: The software industry produces vast amounts of writing but very little of it is good, and curating the best examples serves both as a reward for quality and a rebuke of the mediocre norm.
